metadata.author: LivingPatch Contributors
metadata.description: Southern Catalpa tree and Sphinx moth species with their ecological relationships

species.functional_description: Medium-sized deciduous tree with large heart-shaped leaves and showy white trumpet flowers with purple spots and yellow throat. Flowers in spring attract pollinators and hummingbirds. Produces long thin seed pods (cigar-like) in fall. Native to southeastern US but naturalized in PA. Valuable as ornamental and host plant for Catalpa Sphinx larvae.

life_stages.name: Seedling
life_stages.description: Young tree establishing from seed; rapid growth if moist

life_stages.name: Sapling
life_stages.description: Immature tree; gradually develops characteristic crown shape

life_stages.name: Mature
life_stages.description: Full-grown tree with broad crown; 30-50+ feet tall

life_stages.name: Flowering
life_stages.description: Showy white trumpet flowers with purple and yellow markings

life_stages.name: Fruiting
life_stages.description: Long slender seed pods (8-20 inches) develop after flowering

species.functional_description: Large sphinx moth (hawk moth) with wingspans 3-3.5 inches. Adults are nocturnal and attracted to lights. Gray-brown coloration provides camouflage on tree bark. Larvae (caterpillars) are distinctive yellow-green with dark bands and two tentacle-like filaments on head. Obligate host plant is Catalpa. Larvae can defoliate trees but rarely cause serious damage. Known locally as 'catalpa worms' — used as fish bait.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Small white egg laid on Catalpa foliage

life_stages.name: Caterpillar
life_stages.description: Yellow-green with dark bands; distinctive head tentacles (filaments)

life_stages.name: Pupa
life_stages.description: Brown pupae overwinter in soil or leaf litter

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Gray-brown sphinx moth; nocturnal; attracted to lights

species.functional_description: Large sphinx moth similar to Catalpa Sphinx but with more wavy lines on wings (hence the name). Wingspan 3-3.5 inches. Adults are nocturnal. Host plants include Fringe tree and occasionally other plants. Less specifically tied to one host compared to Catalpa Sphinx. Important part of nocturnal insect community.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Eggs laid on host plant foliage

life_stages.name: Caterpillar
life_stages.description: Green caterpillar; larvae feed on host plant leaves

life_stages.name: Pupa
life_stages.description: Pupae overwinter in soil or leaf litter

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Sphinx moth with wavy wing patterns; nocturnal

symbiosis.notes: Catalpa Sphinx Moth larvae are obligate on Southern Catalpa. Caterpillars feed on foliage; can cause significant defoliation in heavy infestations but tree usually recovers.

relations.notes: Both members of Sphingidae (sphinx/hawk moth) family. Similar size, nocturnal behavior, and flight characteristics. Important pollinators despite herbivorous larvae.
